food_name: Sayur Lodeh (Mixed Vegetable in Coconut Milk Curry)
region_of_origin: Java, Indonesia — widely adopted in Singapore Malay cuisine
food_subgroup: Mixed vegetable and tofu in light coconut milk broth

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Sayur Lodeh. Closest parent is mixed vegetable coconut milk curry (vegetable stew category). No exact URI available; null assigned pending FoodOn expansion.
ontology.foodex2_description: Mixed vegetables in light coconut milk broth with lemongrass and turmeric (Javanese-Malay sayur lodeh)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0VEG is the closest FoodEx2 parent for mixed vegetable dishes; no specific code exists for sayur lodeh.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Sayur Lodeh as of 2026.

main_ingredients: dried shrimp or shrimp paste (belacan, optional)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Sayur Lodeh is one bowl of approximately 300g, as commonly served at Malay hawker stalls and during festive occasions such as Hari Raya alongside lontong (compressed rice cakes).

nutrient_source.title: Sayur Lodeh — HPB Singapore Foodo; cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Regional Food Composition Table
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Regional Food Composition Table
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Sayur Lodeh, cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA Table for micronutrient validation. Copper, manganese, and vitamin K values supplemented from enriched JSON with confidence 0.9 where HPB data was not available for these micronutrients.

gi_source.title: University of Sydney GI Database — analogous estimate for mixed vegetable coconut milk curry (vegetable-dominant, low carbohydrate 5.5g/100g)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Sayur Lodeh in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 38 is estimated from the closest analogous food: vegetable-dominant mixed dishes with coconut milk and low carbohydrate content (~5.5g/100g). The high dietary fibre from mixed vegetables and fat from coconut milk both attenuate glycemic response. GL per 300g serving = (38 × 5.5 × 3) / 100 = 6.27, rounded to 6.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 195mg/100g; a 300g bowl delivers approximately 585mg sodium, primarily from shrimp paste (belacan) if used
hypertension.guidance: Sayur Lodeh is among the lower-sodium Malay curries (195mg/100g), but patients with hypertension should request preparations without belacan or dried shrimp to reduce sodium intake, and limit portion to one bowl (300g, ~585mg sodium) to stay within the recommended daily limit of 2000mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~38), very low glycemic load per bowl (~6.3), and high dietary fibre (1.8g/100g) from mixed vegetables
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sayur Lodeh is an excellent Malay curry choice for patients with type 2 diabetes due to its very low GI (~38), very low GL per 300g bowl (~6.3), and vegetable-dominant composition with minimal starch, making it suitable as a regular side dish without significant postprandial glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 4.5g/100g from coconut milk; a 300g bowl provides 13.5g saturated fat, which is significant relative to the 20g/day recommended limit
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should be mindful of the saturated fat content from coconut milk (4.5g/100g; 13.5g per 300g bowl); choosing a reduced-coconut-milk preparation or limiting portion size is advisable, while the high vegetable fibre and low cholesterol (0mg/100g) are beneficial for cardiovascular health.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High vitamin C (12mg/100g) and folate (22mcg/100g) from mixed vegetables support anti-inflammatory response and joint health
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Sayur Lodeh provides meaningful vitamin C (36mg per 300g bowl, ~40% of the 90mg/day RDA) and folate from its vegetable content, which support anti-inflammatory pathways relevant to rheumatoid arthritis management, though the saturated fat from coconut milk should be monitored.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sayur Lodeh is a light, mildly spiced mixed vegetable curry simmered in diluted coconut milk, originating from Javanese cuisine and widely adopted in Singapore Malay cooking. The dish typically features long beans, cabbage, carrots, eggplant, chayote, and tofu puffs cooked in a fragrant golden broth seasoned with lemongrass, galangal, candlenuts, turmeric, shallots, and garlic, sometimes enriched with dried shrimp or shrimp paste (belacan) for umami depth. Unlike thick coconut curries such as rendang or opor, sayur lodeh uses a lighter, more diluted coconut milk broth — the vegetables are simmered until tender but retain their texture and colour. It is the quintessential vegetable companion to lontong (compressed rice cakes) and is a staple at Malay hawker centres, nasi padang stalls, and during festive occasions such as Hari Raya. Nutritionally, sayur lodeh is one of the most balanced Malay dishes: low in calories (82 kcal/100g), low GI (~38), low glycemic load per bowl (~6.3), moderate in protein (3.5g/100g) from tofu, and rich in dietary fibre (1.8g/100g) and vitamin C (12mg/100g) from the mixed vegetables. The saturated fat from coconut milk (4.5g/100g) is the primary nutritional caution for cardiovascular patients. Sodium is moderate at 195mg/100g, making it one of the lower-sodium Malay curries. It is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (primary approved source for Singapore dishes) with cross-reference to FAO/INFOODS SEA Table. Core macros and most micronutrients carry confidence 1.0. Copper, manganese, and vitamin K carry confidence 0.9 from enriched JSON. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework with documented basis.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for sayur lodeh not available in HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA Table as of 2026-04-13. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB source for this dish. trans_fat_g is confirmed 0.0 per HPB source. cholesterol_mg is confirmed 0.0 per HPB source (no animal fat in standard preparation; shrimp paste contributes negligible cholesterol). vitamin_b12_mcg is confirmed 0.0 per HPB source (plant-dominant dish; shrimp paste if used contributes negligible B12 at this dilution). vitamin_d_mcg is confirmed 0.0 per HPB source. Copper (0.05mg/100g), manganese (0.12mg/100g), and vitamin_k (15mcg/100g) values sourced from enriched JSON with confidence 0.9; not directly available in HPB primary source. GI of 38 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database framework for vegetable-dominant mixed dishes with coconut milk and low carbohydrate content (5.5g/100g); no direct GI entry for sayur lodeh exists in the database. Sodium content varies by preparation — versions with belacan (shrimp paste) will have higher sodium; versions without will be lower. The 195mg/100g figure reflects a standard hawker-centre preparation.
